data.title: Genco Shipping Non-GAAP EPS of $0.40 beats by $0.03, revenue of $77M beats by $0.63M
data.description:
• Genco Shipping press release (GNK): Q4 Non-GAAP EPS of $0.40 beats by $0.03.
  • Revenue of $77M (+16.5% Y/Y) beats by $0.63M.

  • Genco declared a cash dividend of $0.50 per share for the fourth quarter of 2025. The Q4 2025 dividend is payable on or about March 18, 2026 to all shareholders of record as of March 11, 2026.

  • EBITDA for the three months ended December 31, 2025 amounted to $40.1 million compared to $32.6 million during the prior year period. During the three months of 2025 and 2024, EBITDA included other operating expenses, loss on sale of vessels, as well as gains on fuel hedges. Excluding these items, our adjusted EBITDA amounted to $42.0 million and $32.7 million, for the respective periods.

    Financial Review: Twelve Months 2025

    The Company recorded a net loss of $4.4 million or $0.10 basic and diluted net loss per share for the twelve months ended December 31, 2025. This compares to net income of $76.4 million or $1.77 and $1.75 basic and diluted earnings per share, respectively, for the twelve months ended December 31, 2024.

data.title: Select Energy Services GAAP EPS of $0.00 beats by $0.01, revenue of $346.5M beats by $25.65M
data.description:
• Select Energy Services press release (WTTR): Q4 GAAP EPS of $0.00 beats by $0.01.

data.title: Compass Pathways launches proposed $150 million public offering
data.description:
• Compass Pathways (CMPS) on Tuesday said that it has launched a proposed public offering of $150 million of American Depositary Shares (ADSs), each representing one ordinary share, and in lieu of ADSs, pre-funded warrants to purchase ADSs for certain institutional investors.
  • The company said it expects to grant underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional $22.5 million of ADSs at the public offering price, less underwriting discounts and commissions.
  • Compass Pathways said the proposed offering is subject to market and other conditions, and there can be no assurance regarding whether or when the offering may be completed, or its actual size or terms.
  • CMPS -7.35% after hours to $7.05.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Getty Realty launches 4 mln-share common stock offering
data.description:
• Getty Realty (GTY) said on Tuesday it has launched an underwritten public offering of 4 million shares of its common stock in connection with forward sale agreements.
  • The company said it intends to grant underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 600,000 shares.
  • J.P. Morgan and Wells Fargo Securities are acting as book-running managers for the offering.
  • Getty Realty said it expects to enter into forward sale agreements with J.P. Morgan Securities LLC and Wells Fargo Securities, LLC, under which the forward purchasers or their affiliates are expected to borrow and sell an aggregate of 4 million shares to the underwriters, or up to 4.6 million shares if the option is exercised in full and additional forward sale agreements are executed.
  • The company said it will not initially receive any proceeds from the sale of shares by the forward purchasers.
  • It expects to physically settle the forward sale agreements and receive proceeds, subject to adjustments, within about one year.
  • It plans to use net proceeds to fund property acquisitions, repay borrowings under its revolving credit facility, and for working capital and other general corporate purposes.
  • GTY -1.97% after hours to $33.19.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Tactile Systems Technology GAAP EPS of $0.46 beats by $0.02, revenue of $103.6M beats by $9.8M
data.description:
• Tactile Systems Technology press release (TCMD): Q4 GAAP EPS of $0.46 beats by $0.02.
  • Revenue of $103.6M (+21.0% Y/Y) beats by $9.8M.

  • Operating expenses in the fourth quarter of 2025 increased $10.4 million, or 20%, to $62.2 million, compared to $51.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.

    Operating income was $18.8 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared to $12.5 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.

    Income tax expense was $8.8 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared to $3.3 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.

  • Weighted average shares used to compute diluted net income per share were 23.0 million and 24.5 million for the fourth quarters of 2025 and 2024, respectively.

    Adjusted EBITDA was $22.9 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, compared to $16.2 million in the fourth quarter of 2024.

    Full Year 2025 Financial Results

    Total revenue in the full year of 2025 increased $36.5 million, or 12%, to $329.5 million, compared to $293.0 million in the full year of 2024. The increase in total revenue was attributable to an increase of $19.0 million, or 7%, in sales and rentals of the lymphedema product line and an increase of $17.5 million, or 52%, in sales of the airway clearance product line.

    Net income in the full year of 2025 was $19.1 million, or $0.82 per diluted share, compared to $17.0 million, or $0.70 per diluted share, in the full year of 2024.

    Weighted average shares used to compute diluted net income per share were 23.3 million and 24.1 million in the full year of 2025 and 2024, respectively.

    Adjusted EBITDA was $44.8 million in the full year of 2025, compared to $37.1 million in the full year of 2024.

data.title: Expand Energy Non-GAAP EPS of $2.00 beats by $0.13, revenue of $3.27B

data.title: Krystal Biotech targets global VYJUVEK expansion with Italy launch in H2 2026 while maintaining 90%–95% gross margin outlook
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Krystal Biotech (KRYS) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Krish Krishnan, CEO, stated Krystal Biotech "made meaningful progress across all geographies" in 2025, with added U.S. sales capacity, accelerated demand, and international expansion. The company signed distributor agreements covering over 20 countries and aims to expand to more than 40 in 2026. Krishnan emphasized, "Our goal is to expand to over 40 countries in 2026."
  • The CEO reported that VYJUVEK net revenue for Q4 reached $107.1 million, including contributions from Europe and Japan, and total net revenue since launch is over $730 million. Krishnan added, "Gross margin was 94% for the quarter and 94% for the full year. We continue to expect gross margins in the 90% to 95% range for the foreseeable future."
  • Christine Wilson, Head of U.S. Sales & Marketing, shared that reimbursement approval acceleration has occurred for three consecutive quarters and over 660 have been achieved since launch. Wilson highlighted, "We added over 50 new prescribers in Q4 2025 and have reached over 500 unique prescribers since launch."
  • Laurent Goux, SVP & GM of Europe, reported that more than 90 DEB patients have been prescribed VYJUVEK across Germany, France, and Japan combined. Goux said, "Our launch in Germany continues to build momentum since late August of last year. We're seeing sustained prescription growth and good prescribing breadth."
  • Suma Krishnan, President of R&D, mentioned rapid pipeline progress including registrational trials in NK and DEB eye lesions, and plans to initiate trials for CF and Hailey-Hailey disease later in the year. She noted, "We look forward to sharing updates once we have aligned with the agency and expect to start repeat dosing in the first half of the year."
  • Kathryn Romano, Chief Accounting Officer, stated, "Our final net revenue from global sales of VYJUVEK during the fourth quarter of 2025 was $107.1 million, which includes sales from our Q4 launches in both France and Japan." Romano reported Q4 net income of $51.4 million, or $1.77 per basic and $1.70 per diluted share.

Outlook

  • The company expects to expand global distributor agreements to cover over 40 countries in 2026 and to finalize VYJUVEK pricing and launch in Italy in the second half of 2026. Management reaffirmed guidance for gross margins in the 90%–95% range for the foreseeable future.
  • Guidance for 2026 non-GAAP operating expenses is set at approximately $175 million to $195 million in R&D and SG&A combined, reflecting planned spending on global launches and pipeline development.

Financial Results

  • Net VYJUVEK revenue for Q4 was $107.1 million, marking nearly 10% growth from the prior quarter and 18% over the prior year's Q4. Year-to-date VYJUVEK net revenue reached $389.1 million, an increase of about 34% from 2024.
  • Gross margin for Q4 was 94%, compared to 96% in Q3 and 95% in Q4 2024. Cost of goods sold increased to $6.6 million from $4.3 million in Q3.
  • R&D expenses were $14.8 million, and SG&A expenses were $41.4 million for Q4. Operating expenses included $13.8 million in noncash stock-based compensation.
  • The company ended the year with $955.9 million in combined cash and investments.

Q&A

  • Jiale Song, Jefferies: Asked about U.S. vs. ex-U.S. revenue contribution and the 2026 outlook. Krishnan responded that while ex-U.S. will be the predominant growth driver in 2026, U.S. demand is still accelerating, with nuanced utilization patterns emerging as patients shift to intermittent treatment cycles.
  • Samantha Corwin, William Blair: Asked about compliance rates in EU and Japan, and potential for stock buybacks. Krishnan explained compliance in Europe is similar to early U.S. launch patterns. In Japan, the law requires two-week prescriptions in the first year, which may affect compliance but is expected to normalize by year two. On buybacks, Krishnan said there is no plan to use cash for acquisitions and indicated uncertainty on stock buybacks until more clarity is gained on larger indications.
  • Alec Stranahan, BofA: Inquired about ongoing pricing negotiations in France and Germany and dosing protocol updates in ophthalmology. Krishnan expects German pricing to be finalized in H2 2026 and French pricing in 2027, with accruals ongoing until then. Suma Krishnan stated that study power remains unchanged despite protocol modifications.
  • Yigal Nochomovitz, Citi: Asked for more details on patient numbers in Europe and Japan, and Italy's reimbursement setup. Krishnan said current patient estimates are directional and segmentation is not available until Q1 2026. In Italy, launch will occur once pricing is finalized, with no expected accrual situation.
  • Joshua Fleishman, TD Cowen: Asked about factors for at-home self-dosing and dosing schedule differentiation in ocular DEB programs. Suma Krishnan said home administration and patient feedback were key, and regimens were set for flexibility and convenience. Protocol and statistical analysis plans for studies were finalized after agency discussions.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts displayed a neutral to slightly positive tone, focusing on geographic revenue breakdowns, compliance, pricing negotiations, and clinical study adjustments. Questions were persistent but not adversarial, and several congratulated management on performance.
  • Management maintained a confident and steady tone, emphasizing operational discipline and international expansion. Krishnan used phrases such as "we feel really good about the way it's gotten started" and "fingers crossed, hopefully, it continues to go in the right direction," reflecting cautious optimism. There was no observable defensiveness or hesitation in responses.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst tone remained consistent, while management's confidence was slightly higher due to ongoing global expansion and successful pricing negotiations, particularly in Japan.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter emphasized geographic expansion, pipeline acceleration, and international pricing negotiations, while the previous quarter focused on early launch progress and new label approval in the U.S.
  • Guidance for 2026 non-GAAP operating expenses was introduced, up from the previous year's guidance. Gross margin declined from 96% in Q3 to 94% in Q4, with an explanation attributing this to higher costs for products sold outside the U.S.
  • Management's tone was more forward-looking and highlighted global revenue drivers, with analysts increasingly interested in segmentation and compliance trends across regions. The strategic focus shifted from initial launch execution to scaling and optimizing global operations.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted that revenue may not track linearly with overseas patient additions due to accruals, timing effects, and ongoing pricing negotiations.
  • Compliance risk in Japan during the first year of launch was highlighted, given the legal requirement for two-week prescriptions, which may temporarily affect adherence.
  • Ongoing pricing negotiations in Germany and France could impact revenue recognition and timing.

Final Takeaway

Krystal Biotech is entering 2026 with momentum from global VYJUVEK launches, disciplined capital allocation, and continued pipeline progress. Management sees international expansion, particularly in Europe and Japan, as key revenue drivers, while maintaining high gross margins and investing in registrational studies for additional rare disease indications. The company remains focused on optimizing operational execution and is well positioned to support future milestones with a strong cash position.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: San Francisco Fed president Daly: ‘AI applications are ‘good progress, but not transformative’
data.description:
Mary C. Daly, president and CEO of the Federal Reserve Bank of San Francisco, said artificial intelligence-driven productivity gains could allow the U.S. economy to grow without triggering inflation, though she cautioned that it remains too early to declare the technology transformative.

Speaking at San Jose State University during an event hosted by the Silicon Valley Leadership Group, Daly engaged in a moderated conversation with Bloomberg Tech’s Ed Ludlow about the economic implications of AI adoption.

While acknowledging that productivity growth has recently averaged 2.7% compared to the historical 1.9% average, Daly urged caution in attributing those gains solely to AI.

“We still can see that there’s something going on there. The question isn’t, is it happening? The question is, how long will it persist?” she said, explaining that one-time cost savings from automation differ fundamentally from sustained productivity improvements.

She emphasized that policymakers are looking for technology that delivers “consistently good changes in productivity so that all industries at scale get better.”

Addressing widespread concerns about job displacement, Daly offered a historical perspective on technological change. “No technology ever reduces net employment, not in the history of technologies,” she said. “But it does change what that employment looks like.”

She noted that AI is currently replacing tasks and augmenting workers rather than eliminating jobs outright, though she acknowledged that worker anxiety remains high in the current low-hiring, low-firing labor market environment.

Daly revealed that the Federal Reserve itself has adopted AI for operational purposes, including coding assistance and efficiency improvements, though not for monetary policy or economic forecasting.

In her keynote remarks, Daly drew parallels between AI and electrification, noting that it took nearly a century from Faraday’s discovery to electricity transforming the economy.

She characterized current AI applications as “good progress, but not transformative,” comparing them to replacing a steam engine with an electric motor while leaving the factory floor unchanged.

Daly invoked the lessons of the 1990s, when then-Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan examined disaggregated data and business anecdotes rather than relying solely on aggregate productivity numbers to navigate the computer and internet revolution.

“Monetary policy is a forward-looking business,” she said, adding that the Fed must be “grounded in what we see, but also open-minded to what is possible."

More on the U.S. economy

  • A worsening labor market is still the key risk for the U.S. economy – Goldman Sachs
  • Empire State Manufacturing Index surpasses consensus in February
  • U.S. dollar edges higher as mixed data, policy outlook keep gains in check: Currency Recap
  • Odds near one in four Supreme Court rules in favor of Trump's tariffs
  • Slower grocery inflation doesn't mean cheaper prices
